rows.title: New York General Business Law Article 44-B: Regulation of Automated Employment Decision Tools
rows.summary:
Article 44-B of the New York General Business Law establishes requirements for employers and employment agencies using automated employment decision tools (AEDTs) in New York City, representing a local law enacted by the New York City Council and subsequently codified within New York State's General Business Law framework.

On 6 January 2023, New York City Local Law 144 of 2021 came into full effect following the publication of final rules by the New York City Department of Consumer and Worker Protection (DCWP), having been signed into law on 11 November 2021 with an original effective date of 1 January 2023 that was subsequently delayed pending rulemaking. The law is codified at Article 44-B of the New York General Business Law and applies to employers and employment agencies that use AEDTs to screen candidates for employment or employees for promotion where the position is in New York City.

Article 44-B defines an automated employment decision tool as any computational process derived from machine learning, statistical modelling, data analytics, or artificial intelligence that issues simplified output, including a score, classification, or recommendation, that is used to substantially assist or replace discretionary decision-making in employment screening or promotion decisions. The Article prohibits the use of such tools unless the tool has been subject to a bias audit conducted by an independent auditor no more than one year prior to the date of use. The bias audit must assess the tool for disparate impact across sex categories and race or ethnicity categories as defined by the United States Equal Employment Opportunity Commission. Employers must make the results of the most recent bias audit, including the distribution date of the tool and the date the bias audit was conducted, publicly available on their website.

The Article further provides that employers and employment agencies must notify candidates or employees who are residents of New York City at least ten business days before using an AEDT to evaluate them. That notice must inform the individual of the tool's use, the job qualifications and characteristics it evaluates, and must provide an opportunity for the individual to request an alternative selection process or accommodation. Civil penalties for violations are set at no more than USD 1,500 (approximately EUR 1,380) per violation for a first violation and no more than USD 1,500 per day for each subsequent violation.
